profile.summary: A Junior Graphic Designer supports the creation of visual assets for marketing, brand, digital, or print work. They usually execute against existing brand guidelines, prepare files, make revisions, and turn feedback into polished layouts. Their scope is hands-on production more than strategy, working closely with senior designers, marketers, copywriters, and sometimes external printers or developers.

known_challenges.text: Turning broad or conflicting feedback into clear design changes without losing confidence.
Balancing speed, accuracy, and brand consistency on high-volume asset work.
Building craft skills while still needing close direction and review.
Handling repeated revisions and subjective opinions from non-design stakeholders.
Feeling pressure to prove creative value while doing production-heavy work.

recommended_question_themes.why: Junior designers often depend on feedback quality, and unclear input can slow learning and delivery.
This level is still building fundamentals like layout, typography, hierarchy, and file preparation.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Confidence in presenting and explaining choices
recommended_question_themes.why: They may have good instincts but struggle to speak about decisions with stakeholders.
Design teams often face urgent asks and repeated edits that can drain energy quickly.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Understanding of brand rules and when to apply them
recommended_question_themes.why: Strong execution at this level depends on using guidelines consistently across many assets.

terminology.meaning: How type is chosen, arranged, and styled.
How visual elements are placed on a page.
Visual order showing what to notice first.
Rules for logos, colour, type, and style.
Path-based artwork that scales, versus pixel images.
The goal, audience, and constraints for a piece of work.
A structured review where peers give feedback.
Passing finished files and specs to the next team.
Using AI tools to speed up first versions.
Keeping named, recoverable versions of files.
Being able to explain why a choice was made.
Turning critique into changes without losing confidence.
Designing so more people can use the result.

listen_for: They talk mostly about making requested changes, not yet about owning design rationale.
They mention unclear briefs or scattered feedback as a recurring blocker.
They describe checking brand rules often, which signals conscientiousness and dependence on guidance.
They downplay their work or credit others quickly, which can point to low confidence.
They focus on tools or execution details more than audience, message, or business goal.
avoid: Treating them like a fully independent brand owner before they have enough support.
Giving vague feedback like "make it pop" without concrete direction.
Assuming speed on production tasks means they do not need craft development.
Reading quietness in critiques as lack of ideas rather than lack of confidence.
